DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-19-2024 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT MOBILE PRODUCTS, INC.'s DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Mobile Products, Inc.'s demurrer to the second amended complaint is overruled.

The original and second amended complaint ("SAC") allege that decedent was injured on February 4, 2020. Plaintiffs filed this action on April 1, 2021. On April 7, 2022, plaintiff added LayMor, Inc. as a Doe.

Both sides improperly refer to facts outside of the SAC and explain that after discussions, plaintiff realized that defendant-not LayMor, Inc.-was the proper defendant. To that end, plaintiff allegedly added defendant as a Doe 3 on March 27, 2023 and defendant filed an answer on April 28, 2023. The court's register of actions shows that a filing fee was paid for that Doe 3 request, but does not show the actual Doe 3 amendment. In any event, plaintiff filed the SAC which expressly names defendant.

The original complaint alleges that decedent operated the Lay Mor Broom, it was "defective," created a dangerous condition, and caused the death. (Original Complaint, pars. 8, 12, 13.) The original complaint further alleges that Does were responsible for the "manufacture" of the "instrumentality." (Original Complaint, par. 1.)

The Doe 1 amendment against LayMor, Inc. was proper and the original complaint and subsequent pleadings "(1) rest on the same general set of facts, (2) involve the same injury, and (3) refer to the same instrumentality, as the original [pleading]." (Norgart v. Upjohn Co. (1999) 21 Cal.4th 383, 408-409.)

As noted above, it is unclear whether plaintiff filed the Doe 3 amendment as to defendant. But that is of no moment because when plaintiff filed the SAC naming defendant, it was effectively changing the name of LayMor, Inc. (timely Doe 1) to defendant or making express allegations against defendant (alleged Doe 3). (Hawkins v. Pacific Coast Bldg. Products, Inc. (2004) 124 Cal.App.4th 1497, 1503 ["As a general rule, 'an amended complaint that adds a new defendant does not relate back to the date of filing the original complaint and the statute of limitations is applied as of the date the amended complaint is filed, not the date the original complaint is filed.' [citation] But where an amendment does not add a 'new' defendant, but simply corrects a misnomer by which an 'old' defendant was sued, case law recognizes an exception to the general rule of no relation back."].)

In sum, the relation back doctrine applies and the SAC does not facially demonstrate a time bar. Plaintiff has leave to file the Doe 3 amendment naming defendant to clear up the record.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not Reported. (302/RBU)

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Plaintiffs' "motion for leave to file second amended complaint" is granted. "That trial courts are to liberally permit such amendments, at any stage of the proceeding, has been established policy in this state since 1901." (Hirsa v. Sup. Ct. (1981) 118 Cal.App.3d 486, 488-89; see also CCP 428.50(c).) A proposed amended complaint's validity is not considered when deciding leave to amend. (See Kittredge Sports Co. v. Sup. Ct.
